FOREWORD 



When work first commenced oh ADVANCED DUNGEONS & DRAGONS™ Fantasy Adventure Game, one particular aspect of'.j 
fantasy role playing was foremost in my mind: there was either a general neglect of deities or else an even worse use by 1 
abuse. That is. game masters tended to ignore deities which were supposedly served and worshiped by characters in the 
campaign, or else they had gods popping up at the slightest whim of player characters in order to rescue them from perilous 
situations, grant wishes, and generally step-and-fetch. Obviously, there is a broad ground between these two extremes, and 
that is squarely where I desired AD&D to go. As the various manuscripts were being written, I informed both James Ward and 
Rob Kuntz of the direction which the overall work was to take and then followed up with draft copies of the manuscripts. Thus, 
both authors were well-appraised of the form and content of AD&D. Their work, the DEITIES & DEMIGODS™ cyclopedia, re¬ 
flects the fact that they not only were kept abreast of the game as it developed, but that they also adhered to the concepts 
which were instrumental in making AD&D what it is. DEITIES & DEMIGODS is an indispensable part of the whole of AD&D. Do 
not fall into the error of regarding it as a supplement. It is integral to Dungeon Mastering a true AD&D campaign. Experienced 
players will immediately concur with this evaluation, for they already know how important alignment is, how necessary the 
deity is to the cleric, and how interaction of the various alignments depends upon the entities which lead them. Those readers 
not well-grounded in ongoing campaigns must take my word for all this, although they will soon discover for themselves how 
crucial the deities of the campaign milieu are. 

In general, deities are presented in pantheons. You can select which ones, combinations, or parts of pantheons best suit your 
campaign. Players knowing which gods are "real" in the campaign world are able to intelligently choose to serve one (or 
more) suitable to the character’s alignment, profession, and even goals. Included are major, minor, and almost-gods (demi¬ 
gods). There are also a few "divine" monsters and magic items herein. These are creatures and devices so connected to 
mythology that this work was the only place to properly present them. Not included are those minions of Evil found in 
MONSTER MANUAL and the attendant volumes forthcoming. Diabolical and demoniac deities are important to the campaign 
milieu. Without Evil, what does Good have to strive against? It is no great matter to extract these beings from the other works 
and include them amongst the other deities, however. 



The format used to present the beings in DEITIES & DEMIGODS was actually developed through close consultation with my¬ 
self. It was chosen for clarity and completeness of information. The reader can extract basic information quickly, just by 
glancing at the statistics pertaining to the being in question. Greater detail is then presented in the explanatory paragraphs 
which follow the initial data. It is worth commenting that the strength and powers of the beings contained herein are ap¬ 
propriate to the overall work. Thus, addition of these deities and demigods does not imbalance the campaign. Furthermore, 
characters who become a match for them are obviously to be ranked amongst their number, no longer suitable for daily 
campaign interaction, but to be removed to another place and plane and treated accordingly. 

The authors have prepared exactly what AD&D needed to make it a complete work. They deserve praise for the excellence of 
their effort. It.is also necessary to acknowledge the contributions made by the TSR staff, developmentally and editorially 
alike. The writing of this work was a long and complex project which involved countless hours of research. Both authors were 
willing to step back from it and allow the team at TSR to emend and augment their creation to assure that the whole would 
meet each and every need of DM and player alike with respect to the deities and demigods of the campaign milieu. 


DEITIES & DEMIGODS deserves to be a part of AD&D. It was designed to provide the much-needed information on how to in¬ 
clude these "divine" beings in the campaign milieu, how to have the participants interact with them, and offers a broad 
selection from which to choose. It is with sincere pleasure that I welcome James Ward and Rob Kuntz into the AD&D fold. I 
trust that you will find their efforts most rewarding to your gaming enjoyment.

PREFACE 


This work, as all AD&D works, is an attempt to clarify and produce material for those referees who expect and demand high 
quality information for their campaigns. Within its pages, you will find details and facts that will be highly useful to you as the 
DM and to your player clerics. 

It is not intended as a treatment of world religions and the Tightness or wrongness of their philosophies. It is a simple state¬ 
ment of the historical or literary details man has recorded for all to see. Do not look for a favoring of good versus evil or 
neutrality versus everything else. When historians or authors wrote about a deity in a way that made them powerful or 
feared, that is the way we made them. In the same light, when a being was made too all-powerful (in AD&D terms) we 
scaled them down to make them compatible in game terms. 

The creative processes of this book differ from the other AD&D works in that three-fourths of the information given within was 
drawn from knowledgeable sources rather than from the imagination of the authors. The names of the deities and heroes, 
the weapons and powers they used, and many of their personality traits are plain for everyone to discover for themselves. 
What had to be creatively done dealt with concepts that were purely AD&D in nature. All of the leaders of the pantheons 
were given 400 hit points and the rest were scaled down from there; the relative resistance of beings to blows and magic was 
derived from studies of their battles with natural and supernatural forces; while concepts like strength were easil A assigned 
in the case of deities of strength or war, this concept is less easily applied to the more powerful deities who have no need for 
massive muscles. Alignments were perhaps the hardest AD&D concept to deal with, and the one that will have the most 
debate among the interested users of this work. Beings like Set. Loki and Arioch are easy to classify, but when working with 
the middle-of-the-road deities who were often chaotic but known for consistent kindness, or were rogues of the worst sort but 
very companionable, it became necessary to consider them as a whole to make a judgment. While it will be easy in any 
campaign to change the powers and alignments of those beings whom you differ with, remember that drastic charlges can 
also influence the all-important game balance of any given campaign. 

While DEITIES & DEMIGODS is ideally suited to the task of working deities into an AD&D campaign format, everything has not 
been covered in the book. In the 6.000-year plus span of this work mankind has spent a lot of that time adding to the myths 
dealt with herein. We did not try to encompass everything, and it is silly to assume that the five years or so of research that 
created DEITIES & DEMIGODS could suffice. There are areas that were stressed because they were useful to DMs, whereas 
others were left out for the very same reason. The book should be used as a beginning framework for the DM. Sample it, take 
what is wanted, and start the gods as well as the players in a universe. While DEITIES & DEMIGODS reveals a great many 
divine powers and a great many powerful devices, it is the duty of the DM to add to, change, and otherwise modify the in¬ 
formation on these pages for use in a campaign. 

There is a large list of people that helped in some way with this endeavor, and I want to take this opportunity to mention and 
thank them: my parents, my wife Janean, Brian Blume, Ernie Gygax, Will Niebling, Mike Carr, Kevin Blume, Dave Sutherland, 
and E. Gary Gygax, who created a game that will outlast us all.

DUNGEON MASTERING DIVINE BEINGS 

In AO&D, when the deities deign to notice or interfere with the lives of 
mortal men. it is the Dungeon Master who must assume their roles. DMing 
a divinity presents a far greater challenge than playing the role of a 
merchant, a sage, or an ore. Players will quite naturally pay special at¬ 
tention to the words and deeds of the gods, so the DM must make a special 
effort to understand how to present them. 

First and foremost, deities and divine beings are not merely super-power¬ 
ful humans. They have powers, abilities, and qualities totally unavailable 
to the mortal-bom. (See Explanatory Notes.) Even if a human should, 
through some ultra-potent magic, have his or her abilities raised into the 
20’s (unlikely and inadvisable), that person would not gain those powers 
reserved for the gods. The source of a deity’s godheads is in some way 
connected to his or her earthly worshipers, though in what manner tho 
gods derive this power is a mystery totally beyond mortal (or immortal) 
comprehension. However, it is true that a god’s power often increases or 
decreases as tho number of his worshipers varies. Thus doitios, and clerics 
as their agents, constantly try to increase tho quantity and quality of their 
worshipers. 

Tho statistics givon in this book can bo of groat aid to DMs, but thoy do not 
tell how a doity should bo played. Tho gods aro not lists of armor clnssos, 
hit points and attack forms; treating them ns such reduces them to tho rolo 
of moro monsters. Thoy aro, rather, beings whoso voryprosonco profound¬ 
ly affects tho course of events. Many of them represent elemental or 
natural forces that man can barely influonco, lot alono conquor. 
Introduction of a doity into active play in a game is a stop that cannot bo 
taken lightly. Players must understand who they ore dealing with, and that 
impropor bohavior toward a god can bring swift and diro consoquoncos. 

Gods gonorally havo ogos proportionate with their powor and Importance. 
Most deities simply will not stand for mere mortals behaving beyond their 
station, o.g.. treating gods as oquals, or not showing propor respect in 
other ways. Attempting to coerce or intimidate a god in some way will 
nearly always result in divine punishment. Mortals who meddle with the 
gods are usually dealt with harshly in order to provide examples for others 
who may contemplate such actions. 

Most gods onjoy flattery, but any god with a wisdom scoro abovo 15 will 
know it for what it is. and will generally not allow his or her opinion of the 
flatterer to be altered by the flattery. Deities will usually be able to guess 
at the flatterer's motives. 

In any encounter between characters and a divine being, thoro aro several 
things that the DM must continually keep in mind. The first is the divine 
awe (or horror) inspirod in mortals by most doitios’ extraordinary 
charisma. Even if characters are of sufficient level to avoid the direct 
rosults. tho doily’s pronouncements should carry groat weight. Tho socond 
thing to remember is that most gods’ intelligence and wisdom far exceed 
that of mortals. Thoy can raroly (if over) bo foolod or trickod by moro 
humans. Their great wisdom usually enables them to tell when a mortal is 
lying. In fact, as DM, you can usually assume that if you know why a char¬ 
acter is saying or doing somothing. tho doity would know it as woll. This 
should help to simulate the deity’s superior intellect and wisdom, and im¬ 
press the characters. Gods with intelligence scores above 20 can ofton toll 
what kind of action a mortal is about to attempt just from a few prepara¬ 
tory motions, and knowing this, a god (with his or her superior dexterity) 
can often react to the action before it is completed! 

If a god enters combat (willingly or unwillingly), he or she will always at¬ 
tempt to avoid any situation where the god can be physically defeated. A 
little reflection will show that tho idoa of a doity fighting mortals "to tho 
death” is absurd. The easiest avoidance of combat is the god’s innate tele¬ 
port ability, which onablos him or her to loavo combat ontiroly. or ’’blink’* 
away to a convenient distanco and rosumo combat in a mannor of tho 
god’s choosing (spells, special abilities, etc.). This teleport ability takes no 
time or concentration during battle — tho divinity just wills it to happen. 

If engaged in combat, deities will almost always call upon whatever aid 
they can. Some gods have specific aids or attendants listed in the text. 
Those who do not have creatures listed can still usually summon a retinue 
of appropriate beings from the Prime Material or the god’s plane of origin. 
After summoning aid. many gods will depart the field, leaving their 
retinue to do the fighting for them. (That is. after all. their function — gods

When a DM is deciding whtch gods will be commonly worshiped in his or

MORTALITY AND IMMORTALITY 

AD&D assumes that the anlma, that force which gives life and distinct 
oxlstonco to thinking bolngs. is ono of two sorts: sou/ or spirit. Humans, 
dwarves, halflings, gnomos. and half-elves (those bolngs which con hovo 
a ralso doad or rosurroctlon spoil cast upon thorn) all hovo souls: oil other 
beings that worship deities have spirits. This latter group Includes (but Is 
not limited to) elvos. oros. half-oros. and tho other croaturos spoclflcally 
montlonod In tho NONHUMANS* DEITIES section of this work. Tho DM may 
decide on a case-by-case basis whether other creatures have spirits and

is only a suggostod ono. Individual Dungoon Mastors should uso a differ¬ 
ent systom if thoy find this ono unsultablo. 

Whon a being from tho Prlmo Matorlal Plano dios, its soul or spirit goos to 
ono of the Outer Pianos (Soo THE KNOWN PLANES OF EXISTENCE soc- 
tion.) Selecting which plane the soul or spirit goes to is the province of the

propriate plane would be Elysium. If a being has been faithful to the

will pass into the plane whoro tho doity resides. Moreover, tho soul or 
spirit will go to that pari of the plane most strongly influenced by that

residing there (Olympus, Asgard. etc.). 

Another difference between souls and spirits lies in what happens to them 
when they arrive at their destination. For souls, it Is the beginning of 
eternity; it is on this plane that the soul will remain forever, enjoying the 
benefits or suffering the torments thereof. Spirits, on the other hand, may 
be but temporary residents of the plane. Their rewards and punishments 
are less than that received by souls. At some time In the future, at the will 
of the deity, the spirit can be returned to the Prime Material Plane — rein¬ 
carnated. The new creature will not have any sort of overt memories of its 
former life, nor will its new form necessarily be the same as its old. If an 
elf becomes ovll during his or her life, his or hor spirit would go to ono of 
the evil planes at death. The ruler of that plane might, a century later, de¬ 
cide to send the being back as a Drow. for example; this Draw would have 
the same sort of good-evil choices during life as the elf had (albeit from a 
different viewpoint), and conceivably could go to yet another plane upon

death if he or she did not remain evil and loyal to the master of that place. 
The amount of time that a spirit must spend in a plane before returning to

as ten years to a millenium or more — time isnof important to a deity. 

(Note: The above is only a suggested method for dealing with character 
life-after-dealh. The DM may. of course, use whatever system is most ap¬ 
propriate to his or her campaign.) 

There is a time lag between death and arrival at the plane of destination. 
Although time is meaningless to the soul or spirit, the long journey to the 
proper plane can take 3-30 days of time relative to those in the Prime Ma¬ 
terial Plane. Thus the rationale for the progressive time limit on the raise 
dead spell becomes clear: the farther the soul has travelled on its way to 
its destination plane, the more powerful the cleric needed to summon the 
soul back to its mortal coil. The road through the Astral Plane to their desti¬ 
nation is clearly marked for the dead, but it is not free of peril. Some 
monsters roam the ethereal and astral planes at will, which is why burial 
chambers often include weapons, troasuro, and oven bodyguards to pro¬ 
tect Iho soul on its journey. 

A rosurroction spoil functions in a different manner from a raise dead: the 
cleric literally recalls the soul from the p'ane of Ifs deity back Into Its 
former body, where flesh and blood have been magically restored to the 
bones. As this involves the cooperation of the deity on the plane where the 
soul was, clerics must use extromo caution in employing this spell. If a 
cleric resurrects a being of radically different alignment, the cleric’s deity 
(who gave the cleric this powor) may be greatly offended. Similarly, if a 
cloric rosurrocts a boing of different alignment simply to serve the 
purposos of tho cloric or his or her doity (to extract information, for ex¬ 
ample). tho doity on tho plane whoro tho soul was may bo highly dis¬ 
pleased and may take appropriate action. 

The servants, functionaries, and minions of some deities (demons, devils, 
couatl, ki-rin. titans, and othors) aro actually spirits put into thoso forms for 
the purposes of the doity. It should be noted that the forms listed in the 
MONSTER MANUAL are by no means the only ones these servants can 
toko — somo chaotic doitios rulo pianos whoro no two boings have tho 
somo form! 

Immortality in AD&D doos not confer or imply ovorlasting oxistonco; in 
ossonco what tho gods onjoy is otornal youth. Many of tho gods can. of 
course, alter their forms at will and appear young or old; though this ap¬ 
pearance is sometimes dictated by thoir followors (tho ruler of a pantheon 
should appear older, wisor. and inoro maturo than, for example, a god of 
mischief). Any description of gods as "young" or "old” is at best extreme¬ 
ly relative and has no bearing on their powers. A character who obtains

death through "natural causos" (until ho or sho finally drinks tho potion 
that reverses the effects of all the previous ones), but can still be slain by

When deities or their minions travel to planes other than thoir own. they 
aro mystically anchored to their homo piano by a metaphysical "silver 
cord”; this is similar to tho ono described for astral spoil . but cannot be 
broken by the Psychic Wind or any other means. This cord is what pulls a

energy expenditure of this is enonnous, and will weaken the returning

Type V and VI demons must rest a century before returning unaided to the 
plane whoro thoy wero slain (see MONSTER MANUAL); demon princes

If any servant or minion of a deity (or even the deity itself) is slain on its

DIVINE ASCENSION 

As study of the various mythologies will show, it is remotely possible for 
mortals to ascend into the ranks of the divine. However, there are certain 
requirements that must be fulfilled before such a thing could happen. 

First, the character in question must have advanced to an experience level 
that is significantly abovo and beyond tho average lovol of advonturo-typo 
characters in Iho general campaign. (This includes all such non-player 
types as military loadors, royal magic-users, etc.) For example, if Iho 
average level of characters in a campaign, both player and non-player, is 
around 5th level, then a candidate for ascension should be something liko 
9th or 10th level. If the average level is something like 15th, then a charac¬ 
ter would have to bo in the realm of 25th-30th level! 

Second, his or her ability scores must have been raised through somo 
mighty world-shaking magic to bo on par with tho lessor domigods. 
(Should such an act be lightly considered, remember that a wish spell is 
tho most poworful magic that mankind can control, and such an avorago 
increase in abilities would litorally tako tho powor of dozons of wishes! 
Each use of that spell weakens the caster and ages him 3 years Into the 
bargain, so thoy aro not easy to como by.) 

Third, the personage must have a body of sincero worshipers, pooplo con¬ 
vinced of his or her divinity duo to thoir witnessing of and/or boliof in tho 
mighty deeds and miracles which he or she has performod (and continues 
to perform). Thoso must be genuine worshipors. honest in thoir adoration 
or propitiation of tho person. 

Fourth, the person in question must be and have been a faithful and true 
followor of his or hor alignment and patron doity. It is certain that any 
deviation will have been noted by the divine powers. 

If all of the abovo conditions have boon mot, and tho character has ful¬ 
filled a sufficient number of divine quests, then the character’s deity may 
chooso to invost Iho person with a cortain amount of divino powor. and 
bring the character into tho ranks of tho god's celestial (or infernal) 
sorvants. This process of ascension usually involves a groat glowing bonm 
of light and celestial fanfare, or (in tho case of thoso transmigrating to tho 
lower pianos), a blotting of tho sun. thunder and lightning, and tho disap¬ 
pearance of the character in a great smoky explosion. Characters thus 
taken into the realms of the gods will serve their patron ns minor function¬ 
aries and messengers. Aftor several centuries of superior sorvico and 
gradual advancement, exceptional servants may be awarded the status of 
demigod, which includos having an oarthly priesthood and tho ability to 
grant spoils (of up to 5th lovol) to tho dornigod’s dories. 

Naturally, ascension to divinity effectively removes tho charactor from tho 
general campaign, as tho person will become a non-playor mombor of tho 
DMs pantheon. Tho section on DUNGEON MASTERING DIVINE BEINGS 
applies in general to tho summoning of these characters as well, as thoir 
patron deity has rnoro important jobs for his or hor sorvants than to con¬ 
tinually send them when called to interfere in worldly matters.

The gods of the Indians of North America were as close to nature as their 
worshipers could make them. The natural world is the most important 
aspect of the Indians' existence. The gods will always prefer to appear in 
the form of a creature of the land. They can. if necessary, appear in human 
form, but such appearances require great energy and may only last a short 
time. 

In all Indian rituals where a cleric wants to control something, he or she 
must have part of it alroady. If tho cloric is summoning rain ho or sho must 
sprinklo wator on the ground in tho process of the spell. If the cleric is cast¬ 
ing a quest upon a being for whatever purpose, he or she must have a part 
of that being for the spell (a piece of hair, article of used clothing, etc.). 
Tho symbolism of a name is very important to the Indians, so important 
that no charm spell will work on an Indian priest without knowledge of 
that priost's namo. All Indian rituals involving demons ordovils roquiro tho 
use of a large fire for control of the creature. 

Rituals rovolve around tho seasons. Tho obbing of one season into another 
is a time of sacrificing important possessions of the tribe. Food, finely 
mado jowolry, woapons used successfully in battle and the like are burned 
at those times for the good of tho tribe in the upcoming season. Indian 
dories are expected to be apart from the tribe daily in meditation with the 
village’s patron deity. Whon groat ovil comes on tho tribe, it is up to tho 
cleric to protect the tribe, either by direct action or personal sacrifice. 

All Indian clerics dress with a number of magical symbols and relics that 
thoy have fortifiod with holy powor. Thoso relics are buried with tho cloric 
in tho ovent of his death. Tho selection of thoso symbols is personal to tho 
cloric. basod on visions ho will havo rocoivod at tho beginning of his or her 
career. One of thoso symbols will always reflect tho patron deity's symbol. 

Young Indinn dories ontor battlo alongside tho rost of tho warriors of tho 
tribe, while the older clerics attempt to enlist the help of the tribal gods for 
tho battlo. It is tho job of tho tribal priest to make tho warpaint of tho 
warriors and bloss tho mon and woapons before battle. 

The tont or lodge of tho cleric(s) is a place taboo to the rest of the tribe and 
supposedly guarded by strong spirits. Worship of most deities is hold in tho 
open. 

SACRED BUNDLE 

With tho help of a tribal cloric and tho advice of on Indian spirit that has 
been summoned for this purpose, a warrior can make a sacred bundle. 
This thing could bo mado out of anything that tho summoned spirit 
indicates is sacrod to that Indian. It gives tho owner tho following benofits: 
+ 2 on all saving throws; the warrior is surprised only on a roll of 1 in 6; he 
has an armor class of 2; and one point is subtracted from each die of 
damage done to tho being in battle. 

There ore always from 5 to 10 items in a bundle, and the summoned spirit

(thus proving the worthiness of the supplicant). Things like a rattle from a 
cave of giant snakes, a feather from a high nesting giant eagle, or the hair 
of 13 enemies killed in battle are the type of items that go into a sacred 
bundle. When all of the items have been acquired, the priest of the tribe 
must be brought; he will demand that the last offering placed in the bag 
bo of his choosing. This thing is always something that tho priest can use a 
part of for his own purposes.

The Celtic mythology is by no means confined to the British Isles. The This god is the ruler ol a very loose pantheon of gods. His two primary at- 


beings listed are all in human form, unlike some of the other pantheons in 
this work. They all have spheres of influence and these spheres are areas 
of control for tho deities. Any major manipulations of these areas by 
humans or other life forms will cause the god or goddess in question to 
tako an interost (in force) and attempt to put a stop to it. 


tributes are the ability to separate himself into 12 distinct and powerful 
entities. All 12 are fully aware and mobile, but 11 are ethereal in nature 
and roam the earth with unlimited range, constantly supplying information 
to Dagda. These beings have all tho qualities of the original, but they must 
stay in the ethereal state at all times. 


The clerics of most of these deities are druids, which are fully detailed in 
PLAYERS HANDBOOK. All religious services are performed by these druids 
in woodod areas made holy by tho planting of mistletoe and holly. In 
these areas are deep natural wells or dug pits where sacrifices are thrown. 
Human sacrifices are made 4 times a year. These human sacrifices are 
made on Novombor 1 (called Samain) celebrating winter’s start; February 
1 (called Imbalc) celebrating winter’s leavetaking; May 1 (called Beltane) 
colobrating spring’s planting; and August 1 (called Lugnasad) celebrating 
tho timo of harvest. Condemned criminals are typical sacrifices. 


His other attributes include unlimited use of shape change, telepathy, and 
legend lore. He is very fond of walking the earth in tho form of a shabbily

The Aztoc and Maya presented the world with an interesting set of closely 
related gods, goddesses and creatures that have a moral background simi¬ 
lar to that of other ancient mylhoi. The beings are just as evil or just as 
good; tho difference seems to bo that they are not moved by anything re¬ 
sembling human thoughts and feelings, unlike the deities of other mythoi. 
They act only upon their own inscrutable motives. 

Those beings are said to have come from tho stars, and their “plane of 
origin" is not the same as other mythologies. For the purposes of this work, 
we will assume that these gods come from the Prime Material Plane of a 
parallel universe. Commune or gate spells used by the clerics of these 
deities will only connect with this parallel universe and (ho plane whore 
their gods dwell. There is no way that others can summon these deities if 
thoy do not know tho spocial coromonlos usod in contacting thorn. Also, 
those deities’ unusual naturo makos it impossible for them to travel on any 
pianos but tho ones that thoy have worshipers on. 

Tho dories of this mythos aro tho olito of tho populace, and ovon tho low- 
ost lovols have nbsoluto authority ovor any of tho peasants. Along tho 
samo linos, any cleric of a higher levol may give orders to lessor dories of 
the same deity with complete freedom. Though all sects must usually work 
together, there is much clandestine infighting between groups for fol¬ 
lowers among tho rich and pooraliko. 

Rituals are performed every 20 days, and sacrifices take many forms, from 
food In tho spring and fall seasons to humans boforo and aftor battlos and 
during tho rainy winter months. The public is forcod to attend those rituals, 
and they are always held at the temple of the appropriate god. All temples 
aro built on the stop pyramid dosign. with entrances at the bottom at tho 
four compass points and oast of tho altar at tho top. 

Divine punishment of erring clerics is swift. Small mistakes, like the break¬ 
ing of clorical laws or dofoats in personal combat (duols) rosult in im¬ 
mediate loss of some wealth and/or experience points. Major failures, in 
tho form of lost battlos. failod quests, or alignment changos. will cause a 
cloric to bo stripped of all possessions and levels and requiro him or her to 
start again from tho first levol of experience. 

First level clerics must choose a compass direction for their own (east, 
wost, north or south), and this is tho direction that thoy pray to. meditate 
towards, and start their quests towards. From then on. any attacks they 
make or spells they cast in that direction will have +1 added to their 
chance of succoss, i.o. a ♦ 1 chanco to hit. or -1 subtracted from a targot's 
saving throw. Whon In combat or other situations whoro tho direction is 
not choson or dictated by circumstances, the DM should roll a 4-sided die to 
dotormino which compass point tho cloric is facing. Clerics of tho oast must 
wear red clothes at all times, clerics of the south must wear yellow, clerics 
of the wost must wear black, and clerics of the north must woar white.

With his great tusks and goggle eyes. Tlaloc's appearance is quite impres¬ 
sive. He wears all black but for a garland of white feathers. 

At each full moon, a priest of Tlaloc sacrifices a child or baby to Tlaloc. 
Once a year, there is a groat festival held in his honor. Numerous babies 
are bought or taken from the populace. These babies are sacrificed to 
Tlaloc. after which the priests cook and eat them. If the babies cry during 
the sacrifice, this is taken as a good sign that rain will be abundant during 
the coming year. 

If the proper rituals and sacrifices have been followed to the letter. Tlaloc 
will usually grant his worshipers the rain they need. However, if he thinks 
his requirements have not been met. the people will be punished. Tlaloc 
has four pitchers of water: one filled with good water to make crops grow 
properly, one filled with water that contains spiders’ eggs and webs and 
causes blight, one filled with water that turns to frost, and one with water 
that rots all fruit.

The Clhulhu Mythos was first revealed in a group of related stories by the 
American writer H. P. Lovecraft. Beginning with "The Call of Cthulhu" in 
Woitd Tales . Lovecraft began referring in his horror stories to a pantheon 
of boings known as the Old Onos, who had descended to Earth from tho 
stars in pre-human times. First worshiped by the non-human races of the 
planot, tho Old Onos woro lator banished or lockod away by tho older 
gods. The elder gods do not enter into the stories much, and their identity is 
a mystery. Thoy loft tho Old Ones woakonod. but not destroyed. Whon 
man appoarod, ho found traces of tho older civilizations and remnants of 
the pre-human races. Religions grew up around the Old Ones and legends 
of their imminont roturn to powor — especially around Cthulhu. Bits of tho 
old loro woro discovorod ond transcribed into books, oxtromoly dangerous 
books. 


Lovecraft's friends (who included Clark Ashton Smith. Frank Belknap Long. 
Robort Bloch. Robort E. Howard and August Derloth), wroto stories that 
"tied in" with tho discovery of pro-human relics, tho revival of ancient 
worship, or tho consequences of finding a "forbidden book" dealing with 
the Old Ones and their secrets. No great effort was made to keep these 
storios consistent with oach other. Aftor Lovocraft's death in 1937. August 
Derleth founded Arkham House publishing company to reprint his works. 
Derleth also wrote a number of stories dealing with Lovecraftian themes or 
based on fragments of Lovecraft's writings. Since then a number of 
younger authors, outstanding among whom are Lin Carter. Ramsey Camp¬ 
bell and Brian Lumley.’have written stories based on the growing Cthulhu 
mythology. 

Most of the creatures presented here were introduced by the earlier 
authors: Lovecraft. Derleth and Smith. Derleth introduced ffie concept of a 
struggle between the Old Ones and the forces of good. Lovecraft's original 
concept was far less sanguine — alt of his gods were evil and chaotic, and 
the best mankind could expect from them was indifference. 

If you have not read any stories in tho Cthulhu tradition, start with Love¬ 
craft himself. Many of his stories are straight supernatural tales and do not 
deal with the Old Ones, but "The Call of Cthulhu". "The Whisperer in Dark¬ 
ness". "At the Mountains of Madness", "The Dunwich Horror", and "The 
Shadow Out of Time" give the flavor of his work. Then read the imitative 
writings of Derleth and the modem writers. Fortunately, most of these 
stories are gathered into collections of Lovecraft's work and published in 
paperback. 

Cults of men, and particularly of non-human creatures, keep alive tho wor¬ 
ship of the Great Old Ones and anxiously await their return to power. Vari¬ 
ous evil magic-users and priests, desirous of superhuman powers, experi¬ 
ment with some of the forbidden books (such as The Necronom/con) and 
occasionally unloash some horror on themselves or their surroundings. 
Merely spoaking tho name of one of the Old Onos results in a 5% chance 
that tho god named will hear, for those doilies are quite attuned to the 
Prime Material Plane. If the god does hear its name spoken, it will appear 
and attoinpt to kill tho being so rash as to speak its name (somo of the 
groator gods will sond minions to accomplish this).

said to foretell the return of the Old Ones from their imprisonment. He 
charms humans, humanoids, and non-magical animals by his mere 


presence (good alignment, save at -*-4; evil alignment, save at-4). Those

The Greek assembly of gods is probably more familiar to most readers 
than others of the groups in this work, because they were woven into a 
literature that has lasted down through the ages. Many of our civil con¬ 
cepts can bo traced from the assumed actions of the gods and their mates. 

Many of the gods of Greece descended from the greater titans, who were 
older boings before the time of mortals and therefore did not need wor¬ 
shipers to make them strong. The worship by man gave power to the sons 
and daughters of the titans of a type that was not known to their elders. 
This enabled them to throw down the titans and gain mastery of the Prime 
Matorial Plane. 

Because of their need for human worship, the Greek gods show all the 
Iraits of the mortals that they claim to be so high above. The gods are high¬ 
ly jealous, onvious, potty, and fly Into blind rages in an instant. 

The first places of worship for the Greek gods were areas of natural 
beauty: a bubbling spring, a grove of oak trees, and the like. As large city 
stales were croatod, largo olaborato tomplos woro built to honor tho gods. 
If clerics of this pantheon livo in a city, they must build or work in temples 
within tho city. If those dories livo in tho country or sparsely populated 
areas, thoy must find a natural sotting for tho worship of thoir doity. 

Only clerics worshiping Poseidon may ride horses; all others must walk or 
rido in wagons or chariots. No cleric may have dealings with the clerics of 
othor socts for any roason (on non-hostilo torms) os this is considorod a 
minor transgression by their deity and punishable by the stripping away of 
tho third and higher lovols of spoils for a lunar month. 

All dories must permanently attach Ihomsolvos to one tomplo. and this 
temple is tho only place whoro thoy will bo ablo to comrnuno and whoro 
they must bo drawn bock to In thoir word of recall spell. Whilo they may 
Irovol from this tomplo (or gladod area if thoy havo choson a moro natural 
setting) they can only receive their sixth and seventh level spells from 
moditntion in thnt area. If that natural aroa or that tomplo gets sacked or 
dospoilod in any way tho cloric losos his or her higher lovol spell abilities 
until tho area is roconsocrntod.

During the Trojan War. Odysseus added to his reputation of craftiness and 
cunning with various strategems. He is credited with the idea, of the 
wooden horse ruse used to take the city. Whsn returning home from Troy, 
Odysseus was blown off course and landed on an island of the lesser 
Cyclopes. To escape from the human-eating Polyphemus. Odysseus had to 
blind the giant. As Polyphemus was Poseidon's child, this act brought 
about Poseidon’s curse and forced Odysseus to wander for 10 years, never 
quite able to return home. 

Some of the dangers Odysseus overcame were: the land of the lotus 
eaters. Circe the sorceress, a journey to Hades, the Sirens, stealing the 
cattle of the sun-titan Hyperion, sailing between the monsters Scylla and 
Charybdis. and enchantment by the nymph Calypso. Eventually Odysseus 
reached his homo of the island of Ithaca, only to find his faithful wifo bo- 
seiged by suitors who were convinced that Odysseus was dead. In a fit of 
rage. Odysseus slew all tho suitors almost single-handedly.

The translation of these beings Into AD&O terms was one of the more diffi¬ 
cult tasks of this work. They were very hard to categorize In the lawful or 
chaotic sonso bocauso of tho dlvorslty of tholr worldly actions. The pan- 
tlioon that rosultod Is a mlxturo of Vedic and Hindu concopts, usually loan¬ 
ing toward tho Vodlc. These gods and goddosses maintain an aloofness 
greater than normal when compared with some of the other pantheons. 

Thoro oro sovornl concopts that aro original with this soctlon that must bo 
dealt with for gaming purposes. The culture that created this mythos also 
created one of the most rigid caste systems ever known, where one Is born 
to tils or hor station In lifo and loarns to live with It. In a campaign under 
tho Influonco of theso gods, a character may bo bom a clorlc, magic-usor 
or a fighter. One does not switch from class to class or overlap Into other 
areas (which usually prevents any characters from having two classes). 
Tho casto systom also modifies tho bohavlor of playors toward ono anothor 
and toward non-player characters. Persons of higher status are to bo 
treated with respect and honor and either obeyed or left strictly alone. 
Those of tho magic-user caste ore deemed superior to all other castes, 
including tho warriors. AH of this is followed strictly by those who obey any 
sort of lawful code. 


There is also a belief that all prayers will bo answered if tho proper sacri¬ 
fices are made to the gods. If the prayer Is not Immediately answered, 
then some impropriety was made and the prayers must bo done again 
(and again) until the desired result Is achieved. 

Worshipers aro identified by marks they wear. For example, tho followers 
of Vishnu have two parallel lines of a special while earth drawn from the 
hair-line to the bridge of the nose, with a perpendicular line connecting 
them at the bottom. All worshipers have marks of this type somewhere on 
their persons. 

Probably tho most difficult concept this mythos presents, at least In AD&D 
terms. Is that of the "avatar". An avatar Is a physical manifestation of a 
deity upon tho Prime Material Plane. An avatar usually has lesser powers 
and a different appearance than the deity It has sprung from. Avatars 
often represent one particular aspect or side of a deity, and may have 
been created to perform a specific function. A deity may have several 
avatars simultaneously co-oxlslont, each ono difforont and uninvolvod 
with tho othors. Vistinu, in particular, has many avatars. 

Temples and cultural trappings within this pantheon vary from one ex¬ 
treme to another. On one hand, there are awesomely huge shrines to 
slnglo gods that are many stories high, and In the same area thoro are tiny 
shrines dedicated to all of the gods that a single worshiper can use. 

Tho doltlos of this mythos aro ofton doscrlbod as having many heads 
and/or arms. This multiplicity Is somewhat representative of the powers of 
tho deity. For example. If a deity has sovon important abilities, ho or she 
will probably have seven arms. This number may vary from one account to 
anothor. Glvon most of tho doltlos' ability to shape chango. It Is only 
logical that Ihoy hove ns many arms (or bonds) as thoy nood.

Corellon Larethian represents the highest ideals of elvenkind: "he” is 
skilled in all the arts and crafts, and is the patron of music, poetry, and 
magic. Corellon is alternately male or female, both or neither. The god is 
also mighty in battle, and is said to have personally banished such demons 
as Lolth from the sunlit Upperworld. Elven lore states that the race of elves 
sprang from the drops of blood Corellon shed in this epic battle. 

Corellon Larethian uses a magical bow; arrows fired from it never miss 
their target and do 2-20 points of damage. "He" also has a +5 magic 
sword made from a star that strikes for 3-30 points of damage, and always 
detects which of the god's opponents are the most dangerous. Any other 
being who picks up this sword will be burned for 10-100 points of damage. 

Corellon's clerics always wear a blue quarter-moon talisman; non-playor 
character elven clerics can attain tho 7th level. Tho elves build no shrines, 
but always use natural geological formations or amphitheaters for places 
of worship. Other elven deities include Rillifane Rallathil (god of nature). 
Labolas Enoroth (god of longevity), and Hanali Colanil (goddoss of 
romantic love).

other giants, can attain the 7th level. Frost giant shamans often placate

Skoraeus is the Living Rock, a huge granite humanoid who lies at the heart 
of the world. He knows everything that happens in areas touching rock by 
listening to the vibrations that are carried to him. 








NON HUM A NS' DEITIES 

Stonebones will never venture above ground, and he cannot even be 
gated there. While touching raw stone, he regenerates 5 hit points per 
round. He can strike with his fists for 6-60 points of damage each. Skoraeus 
can cast one earthquake spell per round, at the 30th level of ability. He can 
also instantly summon 2-20 stone giants to his aid. 

Stone giant shamans cultivate an attitude of indifference and aloofness. It 
is their belief that the doings of lesser mortals should have little influence 
upon the affairs of stone giants. They regard both law and chaos with 
suspicion, and are inclined to repulse the overtures of either. Stone giant 
shamans can attain the 7th level of clorical ability.

The Norso pooplo created a race of gods that woro as grim and savage as 
the people themselves. Their legends were of mighty battles against foes 
that were sometimes more powerful than themselves. We have grouped 
most of the gods, their enemies, and their helpers here for your use. 

The vast majority of gods that wo list are the Aesir. who come from Odin. 
Vili, and Vo’s line. These beings all dwell above the Prime Material Plano 
and visit it often for their own needs. A second group of gods called the 
Vanir are beings of the air. They are to bo allies of the Aesir in the final 
battle of Ragnarok, but are quite separate from the Aesir in that they don’t 

• deal with the livos of men, and they derive their spiritual powers from 
elsewhere. 

- Ragnarok is to be the last epic battle where the forces of good (repre¬ 
sented by Odin and his kind) are to fight the forces of evil (represented by 
Loki and the giants of Jotunheim). In this baltlo. evil will triumph: they will 
destroy all the mightiest of the gods, and they themselves will be 
destroyed to the last evil creature, leaving only a few of the gentlest of 
good beings to foster an even mightier race of Norse gods. 

Bocauso of thoir vory warlike naturo, the majority of thoso gods and god- 
dossos cannot euro beings with clerical cure spoils. Only those notod in tho 
text can do so. The clerical leader of a group will always sacriftce an 
enemy (if available) or a steer (If not) before any given voyage of con¬ 
quest or spocial baltlo of importance. In any human sacrifice, tho victim is 
simultaneously hanged and thrust through tho hoart with a spoar. and tho 
remains are burned on a pyre of oak. 

* 

Norso tomplos are always rectangular halls with the main supports being 
carved oaken images of humans. These areas (where the images of the 
gods are stored) are not worship halls, but places where the gods’ forms 
woro carved and protected from tho elements. For worship, thoso images 
aro taken out every lunar month, and given sacrifices in the form of gold 
and silver articles (which hod to bo both useful and pleasing to the eye). 

Almost the only way to offend the gods is to have dealings with giantkind 
in any way or show sorno act of cowardico in baltlo. Many acts that would 
bo called blasphemous in othor religions (such as swearing and breaking 
on oath to a god. taking o god’s name in vain, or dofacing an imago of 
god) can all be forgiven by success in the next battle. 


LOCATION 

Most of tho Aosir livo in an aroa of tho plane of Gladshoim known as 
Asgard, which is connected to tho Primo Material Plano by Bifrosl. the 
Rainbow Bridge. (Bifrost is kept well hidden, and few mortals have ever 
pierced tho veil of illusion which hides it.) Asgard includes Odin’s court of 
Valhalla, tho various holds of tho gods, and largo expanses of wilderness 
and sea. It is adjacent to tho blighted land of Jotunheim. tho giants' homo, 
an anomalous blot of evil upon the plane ofGladsheim. » 


DWARVES 

Tho Dwarvos woro thought of as Troll-like boings in Norso mythology, and 
are callod such in legends. Dwarvos will be found living underground in 
thoir traditional caves trying to stay far away from all othor forms of intelli¬ 
gent life. They aid tho gods for the power that such aid can bring them in 
the form of returned favors. There are two principal races of dwarves: 
Modsogner and Durin. The Modsogner are able to produce magic items of 
a non-violont naturo and rival all boings in this art. Tho Durin can creato 
magical weapons of tremendous power and often do so for giants and 
gods alike. 

EINHERIAR 

These are all the honored heroes that have been brought from the Prime 
Material Plane to Asgard upon their death. They are always of the 4th 
leyel and abovo, and there are hundreds of 8th and higher level fighters! 


They fight constantly on a practice field that raises them from the dead at 
the end of the day. There is no telling the exact number of this group, but 
the Valkyries have boon picking tho best warriors for thousands of years. 
They will be the army led by the Norse gods to Ragnarok. 


GIANTS AND JOTUNHEIM 

While tho Monstor Manual doals with giants in a vory comprohonsivo 
manner, just as there are many unusual pooplo. there aro many vory un¬ 
usual giants. Norse giants are magically gifted races, and the most power¬ 
ful have clerical cr illusionist powers, as well as magical weapons. 

All of these giants are found in Jotunheim. which is directly attached to the 
pianos of Gladshoim. Tho giants of this land aro crafty and ruthloss whon it 
comes to war. but weak-willed and naive when it comes to everyday deal¬ 
ings with giants of othor types, gods, and mon. Thoy aro known to some¬ 
times invite the gods to fun and games and will sometimes trade with 
them. 

Many of the giants on the Prime Matorial Plano ore descended from or 
havo contact with thoso of Jotunheim. This is particularly truo in tiro caso of 
the frost and fire giants, who regard Thrym and Surtur as thoir leaders and 
deities.

APPENDIX 1: THE KNOWN PLANES OF EXISTENCE 

There exists an infinite number of parallel universes and planes of exist¬ 
ence in the fantastic "multiverse" of ADVANCED DUNGEONS & DRAG¬ 
ONS. All of these "worlds" co-exist, but how "real” each is depends en¬ 
tirely upon the development of each campaign referee. The charts and ex¬ 
planations which follow show only the various planes tied to that of nor¬ 
mal existence. The parallel universes are not shown, and their existence 
might or might not bo actual. 

THE INNER PLANES 
Tho Primo Matorial Plano 

The Primo Matorial Plano (or Physical Plano) is at tho "cento r" of tho Innor 
Planes. It housos tho universe and all of its parallels. It is tho piano of 
Torra, and your campaign, in all likelihood. Tho Prime Matorial is bounded 
or pormoatod by all of tho Innor Pianos and tho Astral Plano. Tho Primo 
Material Plane is made up of the four elements plus positive and negative 
onorgy. 

The Positive Material Plane 

This is a placo of onorgy and light, tho place which is tho sourco of much 
that is vital and active, tho power supply for good. Any creature from tho 
Prime Material that tried to enter the Positive Material Plane (e.g. from lire 
Ethoroal Plano) would probably bo Instantly consumod by tho poworlul 
onorgios Ihoro. 

The Negative Material Plano 

This Is a placo of antl-mattor and nogntlvo force, tho sourco of powor for 
undond, the onorgy area from which ovil grows. No ono is certain what 
typos of croaturo may exist on tho Nogativo (or Posilivo) Matorial Plano. 
Any croaturo from tho Primo Matorial Plano would probably bo complotoly 
drained of all life and enorgy. 


The Elemental Planes 

Tho planes of Air. Earth. Fire and Wafer "surround" tho Primo Matorial 
Plane. Fig. 1 shows one way of visualizing their relationship to each other 
and the other Inner Pianos. Tho Elemental Planes are represented by tho 
band that surrounds the Prime Material Plane(s). Don't be fooled by the 
apparont sizos of tho pianos represented in Fig. 1. There are an infinite 
number of parallel Prime Material Planes, and each and every one of 
these are bounded by the Elemental Planes, so the Elemental Planes are 
themselves infinito, in effect. Fig. 2 shows ono way of visualizing the 
Elomonlal Pianos' relationship to oach other. It is possible to actually phy¬ 
sically move from ono Elomonlal Plano to another by moving through tho 
Para-Elemental Pianos. 

The Elemental Planes are the homes of many different kinds of elemental 
creatures, and some of them have tho ability to travel to the Primo 
Matorial Plano. Thoro is probably more traffic botwoon tho Primo Matorial 
and tho Elemental pianos than botwoon any othors. This is usually oilhor 
diroct Iravol (through gatos or summoning) or by way of tho Ethoroal 
Plano. Thoro oro also noxlal points in distant, out-of-the-way placos on tho 
Prime Material Plane that lead directly to the Elemental Planes. These 
noxial points would most likoly bo found in tho doopost ocoan (to tho 
Plane of Water), high in the atmosphere (to the Plane of Air), far under¬ 
ground (to tho Plano of Earth), or in an actlvo volcano (to tho Plano of 
Fire). Temporary noxial points may also be established occasionally, such 
ns an opening to tho Elomonlal Plano of Firo In tho middle of a raging for¬

PLANAR TRAVEL 

The purpose of this section is to sum up all of the information that has been 
stated or implied about planar travel. First, travel among the Inner Planes: 
this is usually dono by moans of tho Ethoroal Plano, as it pormoatos all of 
tho Inner Planes. (It is not possible for creatures on the Outer Planes to "go 
ethereal".) As with all planar travel, it is also possible to pass directly from 
ono plane to another by moans of "gates", thereby avoiding ethoroal 
travel. A gate may be formed by a magic spell (such as a gate spell, or 
con/ure elemental, which forms a direct opening between the planes) a 
magic itoin (o.g. an amulet ol tho pianos, a well ol many worlds, a cubic 
gate, or any of the various elemental-summoning devices), or divine inter¬ 
vention. Thoro aro also diroct nexial points connecting the Prime Matorial 
and Elemental Planes. 

Travel to and from the Outer Planes is usually by means of gates or via the 
Astral Plano. (Note that tho Astral Plane can only be entered from the 
Prime Material Plane or one of the "first layers" of the 17 Outer Planes.) If 
a being is gated to ono of tho Outer Pianos, ho comes comploto with body 
and possessions, leaving no part of himself behind. However, if a being 
astrally projocts and travols tho Astral Plano to ono of tho Outor Planes, ho 
loavos his matorial body bohind on tho Primo Matorial Plano, connoctod to 
his ossonco by a silver cord. If his material body is destroyed or tho silvor 
cord Is snapped while he Is In the Astral Plane, the being Is permanently 
and irrevocably killed. If he moves to one of the Outer Planes from the 
Astral Plano, a body torrns around him, but it Is dovoid ot possessions. Both 
bodlos aro now connoctod by tho silvor cord, and if either body is 
dostroyod, tho being will continue on in tho remaining body (reluming in¬ 
stantly to the Prime Material if the Outer Plane body is killed). If Ihe silver 
cord is snappod on tho Astral Plano, tho unoccuplod body dies.
